texts.orig: Membrane gas solvent contactors for CO2 capture in a post-combustion scenario are able to combine the high selectivity for CO2 of a solvent absorption process within the compact module of a membrane (Simons et al., 2009; Zhao et al., 2016). This is achieved by having a semi-permeable membrane separating the solvent and gas phases, and hence the mass transfer area per unit volume is signi fi cantly higher than a traditional solvent column. Furthermore, the two phases ' fl ows are strictly controlled within the membrane module to avoid fl ooding and entrainment. These advantages have made membrane contactors attractive alternatives to traditional solvent absorption processes, with a number of pilot plants trialled (Scholes et al., 2014, 2012). The majority of membrane contactor research for CO2 capture has focused on the absorption process using both porous and non-porous membranes with various solvents (Kosaraju et al., 2005; Scholes et al., 2015a). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane gas-solvent contactors are potentially more e ffi cient at undertaking solvent regeneration in carbon capture applications compared to traditional desorber columns. This potential is investigated here through modelling of two experimentally reported membrane contactors based on Te fl on AF1600 and polydimethylsiloxane active layers, by a simple approximate model and a one-dimensional mass transfer model. The respective membrane contactors could be operated at temperatures below that corresponding to the vaporisation of the solvent, due to the separation of the solvent and gas phase by the membrane. This required a steam sweep operating under vacuum conditions. The calculated module length for the Te fl on AF1600 membrane varied between 8.9 -70.2 m with decreasing regeneration temperature. This increase in required length was due to reductions in overall mass transfer coe ffi cient and mass transfer driving force as temperature is lowered. It was determined that at 110 °C a PDMS contactor of length 1.1 m was required to regenerate the solvent, achievable with commercial modules. A comparison of the equipment volume footprint, known as process intensi fi cation, revealed that both the Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS membranes required a lower volume than a standard packed column when operating at temperatures above 90 °C. Temperatures higher than 95 °C also designated the transition above which membrane contactors have a lower energy duty than the corresponding solvent column approach. This energy duty is a trade-o ff between the reduction in latent heat required to produce CO2 and regenerate the solvent at lower temperatures, countered by the work duty of the vacuum pump needed to operate the steam sweep at pressures below atmospheric. The investigation demonstrated that membrane contactors are a viable alternative technology for solvent regeneration.

This is important in many carbon capture situations where space is limited, such as o ff -shore platforms. Regeneration of the CO2 loaded solvent (i.e. stripping) through a membrane contactor is less reported in the literature, though a number of recent studies have demonstrated the feasibility (Dibrov et al., 2014; Khaisri et al., 2011; Koonaphapdeelert et al., 2009; Simioni et al., 2011). The major advantage of membrane contactors in solvent regeneration is the ability to remove the CO2 from the solvent below the vaporization temperature of the loaded solvent. This can be achieved through either the gas phase being under vacuum or applying a sweep gas, generally steam. This enables the CO2 partial pressure above the solvent to be reduced below the vapour-liquid equilibrium, resulting in CO2 liberation from the solvent, and hence CO2 desorption without full vaporization of the solvent. texts.label: text
texts.orig: A number of reliable mass and energy transfer models have been developed for membrane contactors undertaking CO2 absorption. Here, two models are presented and utilised for CO2 desorption from a solvent; a simple mass transfer approximation adapted from packed columns and a more detailed one-dimensional model for mass transfer through hollow fi bers. The contactors modelled are based on a nonporous composite membranes consisting of Te fl on AF1600 on porous polypropylene (PP) and an asymmetric polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), given that their performance in the literature has previously been reported for solvent regeneration over a range of temperatures (Scholes et al., 2016, 2015b). The loaded solvent undergoing regeneration is 30 wt% monoethanolamine (MEA), given it is the generic standard for CO2 capture and the properties of the loaded and non-loaded solvent are well known. The evaluated modelled performance of these two contactors will provide insight into the viability of membrane contactors to achieve process intensi fi cation for CO2 desorption.

Correlations exist that can predict the mass transfer coe ffi cient within the gas and solvent boundary layers as well as the porous support and membrane layer (Scholes and Shen, 2018). However, here the overall mass transfer coe ffi cients determined for the two membranes in laboratory measurements are used and provided in Table 1. For these membrane contactors, the solvent is on the shell side and the CO2 product gas on the lumen side of the hollow fi bers (Fig. 1). This assumes that there is a sweep gas applied to the gas phase side of the contactor to prevent concentration polarisation and minimal gas phase boundary layer. The overall mass transfer coe ffi cient for the Te fl on AF1600 membrane are higher than the PDMS membrane for the temperature range studied because that system was measured at a higher Reynolds numbers for the solvent phase. This is strongly associated with the respective packing density of the module and solvent fl owrate and highlight that the solvent boundary layer provides a signi fi cant resistance to mass transfer. In the simulations applied here the sweep gas is assumed to be steam because it can readily be separated from CO2 through condensation, however in the laboratory experiments the sweep gas was N2. Hence, the sweep gas conditions on the lumen side of the simulations were designed to have the same Reynolds numbers as those achieved in the laboratory measurements for the two membranes to counter this variation. To prevent signi fi cant steam condensation at temperatures below 100 °C, the sweep gas is operated under a partial vacuum, the con fi guration of which has previously been studied by (Wang et al., 2014).

texts.orig: 2.3. Simulation details
texts.text: 2.3. Simulation details

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The details of the Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS membrane modules are provided in Table 2, based on literature (Scholes et al., 2016, 2015b) and comparable commercial modules currently available. The solvent is 30 wt% MEA with an initial CO2 loading of 0.45, which is reduced through the membrane contactor to a target loading of 0.2. The solvent fl owrate is 150 kg/hr, which is divided between 10 membrane contactors in parallel to ensure a reasonable solvent velocity of between 0.96 and 1.9 ms -1 in each contactor that correspond to Reynolds Numbers between 175 and 564, over the conditions studied. For the Te fl on AF1600 membrane contactor, the solvent temperature at entry to the module is 70, 80, 90, 100 or 105 °C. For the PDMS membrane, the solvent entry temperature is 90, 100, 105 and 110 °C, the last condition is above the boiling temperature of the solvent and hence there is potentially two-phase fl ow in the contactor. The steam sweep is superheated above the saturation conditions for those temperatures and pressure conditions (vacuum) (Fang et al., 2009), which provides the thermal energy. However, the steam pressure is below or at atmospheric to ensure it is low-grade, and hence can be associated with waste steam found in many power stations. The simulations assumed no evaporation of MEA from the solvent phase through the membrane, which may not be valid at the operation temperatures. If MEA evaporation occurred, then the solvent concentration would reduce and the correspondingly CO2 loading would be lower than determined in the simulations. This will impact the driving force, leading to longer contactor lengths than those determined, to achieve the same degree of solvent regeneration. Data used in these simulations is provided in Table 3. The vacuum pressure on the sweep was as low as 0.3 bar to prevent steam condensation (for 70 °C) and corresponded to a variable fl owrate, dependent on temperature, averaging 12.5 L/s. The solvent fl owrate is 150 kg/hr, which is divided between 10 membrane contactors in parallel to ensure a reasonable solvent velocity of between 0.96 and 1.9 ms -1 in each contactor that correspond to Reynolds Numbers between 175 and 564, over the conditions studied. For the Te fl on AF1600 membrane contactor, the solvent temperature at entry to the module is 70, 80, 90, 100 or 105 °C. For the PDMS membrane, the solvent entry temperature is 90, 100, 105 and 110 °C, the last condition is above the boiling temperature of the solvent and hence there is potentially two-phase fl ow in the contactor. The steam sweep is superheated above the saturation conditions for those temperatures and pressure conditions (vacuum) (Fang et al., 2009), which provides the thermal energy. However, the steam pressure is below or at atmospheric to ensure it is low-grade, and hence can be associated with waste steam found in many power stations. The simulations assumed no evaporation of MEA from the solvent phase through the membrane, which may not be valid at the operation temperatures. If MEA evaporation occurred, then the solvent concentration would reduce and the correspondingly CO2 loading would be lower than determined in the simulations. This will impact the driving force, leading to longer contactor lengths than those determined, to achieve the same degree of solvent regeneration. Data used in these simulations is provided in Table 3. The vacuum pressure on the sweep was as low as 0.3 bar to prevent steam condensation (for 70 °C) and corresponded to a variable fl owrate, dependent on temperature, averaging 12.5 L/s. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The calculated length of the respective membrane modules to regenerate the loaded solvent is provided in Fig. 2, as a function of inlet solvent temperature and simulation model. The two models have comparable trends of decreasing contactor length with increasing inlet temperature. The longest contactor lengths occur for low inlet temperatures because of the corresponding low equilibrium vapour CO2 partial pressure at those temperatures. This resulted in a reduced driving force for mass transfer; as such the solvent must transverse a longer length and experience a greater contact area to achieve the regeneration loading target. Increasing the solvent inlet temperature reduced the required contactor length to regenerate the solvent. This is a product of increasing driving force for mass transfer with temperature, coupled with an increase in overall mass transfer coe ffi cients (Table 1)

This di ff erence between the two models for the two membranes is attributed to the more rigorous calculations of the 1D model, which account for packing density, solvent velocity as well as pressure drop, have on the ability to desorb CO2 from the solvent through the membrane. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Te fl on AF1600 contactors required a length of 70.2 m at 70 °C, 16.3 m at 90 °C, which reduces to 8.9 m at 105 °C (1D model) to achieve the required solvent regeneration. The substantial decrease in contactor distance required for the Te fl on AF1600 contactor going from 70 to 80 °C is due to the tripling of the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient (Table 1). The PDMS membrane required a longer contactor length of 21.3 m at 90 °C and 18.2 m at 105 °C (1D model). This di ff erence between the two contactors performance is again due to packing density, with a higher solvent velocity in the PDMS contactor relative to the Te fl on AF1600 contactor for the same volumetric fl owrate. Hence, to achieve the same regeneration target with the higher solvent velocity, and corresponding pressure drop, a longer contactor length was required, even though the speci fi c area of the PDMS module is greater. This behaviour demonstrates the importance of packing density has on membrane contactor performance for desorption. For the PDMS contactor, at 110 °C the required length was signi fi cantly short at 1.1 m, because two phase fl ow was present on the solvent side at this temperature (Scholes and Shen, 2018), dramatically improving the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient (Table 1). Hence, under these conditions the PDMS contactor acted more as a gas separation membrane rather than a conventional gas-solvent contactor.

texts.orig: 3.2. Solvent phase loading
texts.text: 3.2. Solvent phase loading

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The loading in the solvent phase through the pro fi le of the Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS contactors is provided in Fig. 3, at two di ff erent temperatures. The di ff erent distance for the temperature pro fi les correspond with required length to achieve the regeneration loading target. Over 80% of the solvent regeneration occurred within the fi rst 10% of the length of the contactor, with the majority of the length required to strip out the remaining 20% of CO2. This additional length was needed because the low equilibrium vapour pressure at these lower loadings dramatically reduced the driving force for desorption. These pro fi les demonstrate that if the regeneration loading target is increased above 0.2, then the corresponding contactor length required can be reduced dramatically while operating under the same temperature and solvent fl ow conditions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Gas phase composition
texts.text: 3.3. Gas phase composition

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 concentration in the sweep gas along the contactor length (dimensionless) is provided in Fig. 4 for the Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS membranes, as a function of temperature. The sweep gas was countercurrent to the solvent fl ow, and hence the CO2 product exited when dimensionless distance is zero and entered the module at 1. For both membranes the CO2 concentration in the exit gas reduced as the temperature of the process increased. This was a direct result of the increased steam pressure within the sweep gas diluting the CO2 being stripped from the solvent. Hence, at lower temperatures, the steam was under vacuum to prevent condensation, resulting in the liberated CO2 generating a higher corresponding mole fraction in the gas phase. This decreased the driving force for removal of the CO2 from the solvent at lower temperatures, further increasing the required contactor length. Similar to the solvent loading pro fi les, the majority of liberated CO2 was present within the sweep gas in the fi rst 20% of the contactor length, before the sweep exits the contactor module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The required contactor module volume for Te fl on AF1600 membrane intersected the desorber column volume at 90 °C (1D model). This indicates that at temperatures above 90 °C, the required contactor module volume was less than that of the desorber column. This is indicative of process intensi fi cation, where membrane contactors require less volume to undertake the same unit operation as a column. Accounting for the other membrane advantages, such as operational in any orientated and directional con fi guration, then membrane contactors are competitive for solvent regeneration. The intensi fi cation factor for the Te fl on AF1600 membrane at 105 °C was 1.9, correlating to the membrane contactors requiring almost half the volume footprint of a desorber column. For the PDMS membrane, the required contactor volume was less than the desorber column volume over the temperature range studied and hence indicative for this membrane of process intensi fi cation for solvent regeneration at temperatures 90 °C and above. For the PDMS membranes the intensi fi cation factor at 105 °C was 1.4, which is less than the Te fl on AF1600 membrane. These intensi fi cation factors are of the same order as those previously reported in the literature for a desorption system (Albarracin-Zaidiza et al., 2017). At 110 °C, the intensi fi cation factor for PDMS increased to 24, a signi fi cant reduction in the required volume footprint. This intensi fi cation factor was due to the solvent phase being partially vaporized and hence the PDMS contactor is now undertaking gas separation (Scholes and Shen, 2018), rather than standard gas-solvent separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy demand for the membrane contactor process was between two competing duties. Lowering the temperature reduced the energy demand from the steam sweep, as the latent heat requirement reduced as less water is vaporized from the solvent. Countering this was the requirement to operate the steam sweep under vacuum conditions to prevent condensation, therefore as temperature reduced the duty of the associated vacuum pump increased. This trade-o ff resulted in an energy duty minimum at 100 °C for the Te fl on AF1600 membrane and between 100 -105 °C for the PDMS membrane. At temperatures lower than this minimum, the energy duty of the vacuum pump was greater than the energy savings in the latent heat requirements of the process, while at higher temperatures the latent heat demands signi fi cantly outweighs the loss of the vacuum pump duty. The percentage contribution of the vacuum pump duty and reaction duty to liberate CO2 to the overall energy duty of the process is clearly depicted in Fig. 7. This demonstrated that operating at high temperature requires only a small vacuum duty to operate just below atmospheric pressure to prevent steam condensation, with the majority of the energy duty associated with reversing the carbamate reaction to liberate CO2 as well as sensible energy to ensure constant operation temperature. As the steam temperature was reduced the contribution of the vacuum pump increases dramatically as a deeper vacuum was required. The remaining energy duty contribution of liberating CO2 reduces as a % contribution, given heat of reaction is constant irrespective of operation temperature, while the sensible energy reduces with lower temperature to only a fraction of the energy demand. These energy pro fi les are comparable to that previously reported by (Wang et al., 2014), however the magnitude of the energy duties reported here are higher than that study. This di ff erence is associated with a higher concentration MEA solvent and the more rigorous model in this study.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusion
texts.text: 4. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The simulation of two membrane contactors based on Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS for the desorption of CO2 from 30 wt% MEA solvent has demonstrated that this approach has advantages in terms of process intensi fi cation and energy duty compared to traditional desorber column. Two models to simulate a membrane contactor undergoing desorption were investigated, a simple approximation model and a more rigorous one-dimensional model. Both models have the same trend with temperature, of a reduction in required contactor length to undertake desorption, but with discrepancy between their actual values. The more rigorous one-dimensional model was assumed to be more accurate given its dependence on solvent fl owrate and pressure drop. There was an advantageous operating temperature range for both Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS membrane contactors between 90 to 105 °C. Above this temperature and the solvent partially vaporizes and below this temperature the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient and mass transfer driving force are reduced, and the corresponding length of the contactor needed extends dramatically. This temperature range corresponds to a reduced equipment volume compared to a traditional desorber column as well as reduced energy duty relative to a traditional solvent process. This represented a trade-o ff between the reduced steam demand at operating below the vaporization temperature of the solvent countered by the energy demand of the vacuum pump required to operate at steam sweep that does not condensation. This result therefore indicates that membrane contactor technology is potentially a viable alternative for solvent regeneration instead of a packed column confi guration.
texts.text: The simulation of two membrane contactors based on Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS for the desorption of CO2 from 30 wt% MEA solvent has demonstrated that this approach has advantages in terms of process intensi fi cation and energy duty compared to traditional desorber column. Two models to simulate a membrane contactor undergoing desorption were investigated, a simple approximation model and a more rigorous one-dimensional model. Both models have the same trend with temperature, of a reduction in required contactor length to undertake desorption, but with discrepancy between their actual values. The more rigorous one-dimensional model was assumed to be more accurate given its dependence on solvent fl owrate and pressure drop. There was an advantageous operating temperature range for both Te fl on AF1600 and PDMS membrane contactors between 90 to 105 °C. Above this temperature and the solvent partially vaporizes and below this temperature the overall mass transfer coe ffi cient and mass transfer driving force are reduced, and the corresponding length of the contactor needed extends dramatically. This temperature range corresponds to a reduced equipment volume compared to a traditional desorber column as well as reduced energy duty relative to a traditional solvent process. This represented a trade-o ff between the reduced steam demand at operating below the vaporization temperature of the solvent countered by the energy demand of the vacuum pump required to operate at steam sweep that does not condensation. This result therefore indicates that membrane contactor technology is potentially a viable alternative for solvent regeneration instead of a packed column confi guration.
